data.title: Is Invesco (IVZ) Offering Value After Its 102% One Year Share Price Surge
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Invesco's current share price offers genuine value or just reflects short term optimism, it helps to start with what the market has been doing with the stock recently.
  • The share price closed at US$24.81, with returns of 5.2% over the past 7 days, 5.9% over 30 days, a 7.9% decline year to date, and 101.8% over the last year. This raises fair questions about how much of the story is already in the price.
  • Recent headlines around asset managers and capital markets firms have kept attention on how investors are pricing fee based businesses and their sensitivity to market flows. This backdrop helps frame why a stock with Invesco's recent return profile can see shifts in risk perception even without company specific shocks dominating the news.
  • Invesco currently has a valuation score of 4 out of 6, which means it screens as undervalued on several checks. Next comes a closer look at the usual valuation tools, followed by a different framework at the end that can help you see whether those numbers really line up with the story you have in mind.

Invesco delivered 101.8% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Capital Markets industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Invesco Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to earn above the return that equity investors require, and then capitalizes those “extra” earnings into an intrinsic value per share.

For Invesco, the model starts with a Book Value of US$21.89 per share and an Average Return on Equity of 9.47%. Based on analyst inputs, this translates into a stable EPS of US$2.89 per share, compared with an estimated Cost of Equity of US$2.52 per share. The difference is the Excess Return of US$0.37 per share, which is what the model treats as value created beyond investor expectations.

The analysis also uses a Stable Book Value of US$30.54 per share, based on future book value estimates from 2 analysts, to project how long those excess returns might continue and what they could be worth in today’s terms.

Putting these inputs together, the Excess Returns model arrives at an intrinsic value of about US$38.11 per share, which sits 34.9% above the recent share price of US$24.81. On this measure, the stock screens as materially undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests Invesco is undervalued by 34.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 59 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Invesco.

Approach 2: Invesco Price vs Sales

For profitable asset managers, the price to sales ratio can be a useful cross check because fees and revenue tend to anchor long term business value, even when earnings move around more from year to year.

What counts as a “normal” P/S multiple typically reflects what investors are willing to pay for each dollar of revenue, given their expectations for growth and their view of risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually lines up with a lower one.

Invesco currently trades on a P/S ratio of 1.73x. That sits below the Capital Markets industry average of 3.64x and the peer average of 3.05x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Invesco is 1.58x, which is its proprietary estimate of what a “justified” P/S might be after weighing factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, the company’s size, industry, and risk profile. This Fair Ratio can be more tailored than a simple comparison to broad industry or peer averages, which may mix very different business models and risk levels. On this framework, Invesco’s actual P/S of 1.73x is slightly above the Fair Ratio of 1.58x, suggesting the shares screen as mildly overvalued on this metric.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/S ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 19 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Invesco Narrative

Earlier the analysis mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, and that is where Narratives come in. Narratives give you a simple way to attach your own story about Invesco to concrete assumptions for future revenue, earnings, margins and fair value, then compare that fair value to the current share price to help you decide whether the stock looks expensive or cheap to you.

On Simply Wall St's Community page, Narratives let you set out your view in plain language, link it directly to a forecast and valuation, and then see how that stacks up against other investors. The numbers then automatically update when new earnings, news or analyst estimates are added.

For example, one investor might build a more optimistic Invesco Narrative that lines up with a higher fair value near US$33.21, based on expectations for improving profitability and operating leverage. Another investor might prefer a more cautious Narrative that anchors closer to US$24.00, reflecting concerns about fee pressure and competition. Seeing those two side by side can make it easier for you to decide which story feels more reasonable given your own expectations.

For Invesco however, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Invesco Narratives:

These are not instructions on what to do with the stock. They are two worked examples that show how different assumptions about earnings, fees and competition can translate into very different fair values.

🐂 Invesco Bull Case

Fair value: US$29.32 per share

Gap to this fair value at the last close of US$24.81: around 15.4% below that narrative fair value

Revenue growth assumption: 6.73% decline per year

  • Assumes Invesco can lean on ETF growth, expansion in private markets and digital tools to support earnings and improve margins even while revenue trends are under pressure.
  • Builds in higher profit margins over time, rising to a little above 20%, with earnings reaching about US$1.1b by 2029 and a P/E of 15.1x on those earnings.
  • Relies on cost discipline, share buybacks and balance sheet work to support operating leverage, with the analyst price target of US$29.32 sitting 17.4% above a reference share price of US$24.21.

🐻 Invesco Bear Case

Fair value: US$24.00 per share

Gap to this fair value at the last close of US$24.81: around 3.4% above that narrative fair value

Revenue growth assumption: 3.72% decline per year

  • Starts from concerns that fee pressure, direct indexing and robo advice could weigh on pricing power, particularly in active products, even if assets under management continue to grow.
  • Uses a lower P/E of about 8.8x on projected earnings of roughly US$1.1b by 2028, with margins improving but the valuation multiple compressed compared to both today and the wider Capital Markets industry.
  • Anchors to a bearish analyst price target that was US$17.03 when first modeled, two standard deviations below an earlier consensus of US$22.58, and treats recent fair value moves up to US$24.00 as reflecting better earnings assumptions but still meaningful execution risk.

These two Narratives show how the same company can screen as either attractively priced or slightly stretched once you plug in different assumptions on fee pressure, flows, margins and the P/E you think is reasonable. The useful next step is to see the full set of Community Narratives, compare the inputs to your own view, and decide where your story for Invesco sits between these bookends. data.title: Companies Like DiaMedica Therapeutics (NASDAQ:DMAC) Are In A Position To Invest In Growth
data.description:
We can readily understand why investors are attracted to unprofitable companies. For example, although Amazon.com made losses for many years after listing, if you had bought and held the shares since 1999, you would have made a fortune. But the harsh reality is that very many loss making companies burn through all their cash and go bankrupt.

So, the natural question for DiaMedica Therapeutics (NASDAQ:DMAC) shareholders is whether they should be concerned by its rate of cash burn. For the purpose of this article, we'll define cash burn as the amount of cash the company is spending each year to fund its growth (also called its negative free cash flow). Let's start with an examination of the business' cash, relative to its cash burn.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

When Might DiaMedica Therapeutics Run Out Of Money?

A company's cash runway is the amount of time it would take to burn through its cash reserves at its current cash burn rate. In December 2025, DiaMedica Therapeutics had US$60m in cash, and was debt-free. In the last year, its cash burn was US$29m. So it had a cash runway of about 2.1 years from December 2025. Arguably, that's a prudent and sensible length of runway to have. The image below shows how its cash balance has been changing over the last few years.

Check out our latest analysis for DiaMedica Therapeutics

How Is DiaMedica Therapeutics' Cash Burn Changing Over Time?

DiaMedica Therapeutics didn't record any revenue over the last year, indicating that it's an early stage company still developing its business. Nonetheless, we can still examine its cash burn trajectory as part of our assessment of its cash burn situation. With the cash burn rate up 32% in the last year, it seems that the company is ratcheting up investment in the business over time. That's not necessarily a bad thing, but investors should be mindful of the fact that will shorten the cash runway. While the past is always worth studying, it is the future that matters most of all. So you might want to take a peek at how much the company is expected to grow in the next few years.

How Easily Can DiaMedica Therapeutics Raise Cash?

While DiaMedica Therapeutics does have a solid cash runway, its cash burn trajectory may have some shareholders thinking ahead to when the company may need to raise more cash. Issuing new shares, or taking on debt, are the most common ways for a listed company to raise more money for its business. Many companies end up issuing new shares to fund future growth. By looking at a company's cash burn relative to its market capitalisation, we gain insight on how much shareholders would be diluted if the company needed to raise enough cash to cover another year's cash burn.

Since it has a market capitalisation of US$357m, DiaMedica Therapeutics' US$29m in cash burn equates to about 8.2% of its market value. Given that is a rather small percentage, it would probably be really easy for the company to fund another year's growth by issuing some new shares to investors, or even by taking out a loan.

Is DiaMedica Therapeutics' Cash Burn A Worry?

Even though its increasing cash burn makes us a little nervous, we are compelled to mention that we thought DiaMedica Therapeutics' cash burn relative to its market cap was relatively promising. Considering all the factors discussed in this article, we're not overly concerned about the company's cash burn, although we do think shareholders should keep an eye on how it develops. On another note, DiaMedica Therapeutics has 4 warning signs (and 2 which can't be ignored) we think you should know about.
